title: An epidemic, generation by generation
author: OpenDiscover
blurb: Before the SIR differential equations there was Reed and Frost's classroom model (1928): time moves in discrete generations, and each susceptible either escapes every current infective or doesn't. From a single case in a town of 1,000 the chain of infection builds, peaks as the susceptible pool runs dry, then burns out — the classic single-humped epidemic curve. With R₀=2.5 about 89% are eventually infected, matching the large-population final-size law z = 1 − e^(−R₀z). The discrete, coin-flip cousin of the SIR model, and the seed of modern stochastic epidemic theory.
